confidence.disclosure: Itinerary is reconstructed from Ibn Hawqal's own Surat al-Ard / Kitab al-Masalik wa-l-Mamalik. He recorded only his departure date (15 May 943, from Baghdad) precisely; the dates for individual stops are inferred from internal references in the text and from André Miquel's chronological reconstruction. He almost certainly visited the cities listed below, but whether he actually reached the southern Sahara below Awdaghust or only relayed merchant testimony is debated, so I have included only Sijilmasa and omitted the deeper Sahel.

sources: J.H. Kramers and G. Wiet (trans.), Ibn Hauqal: Configuration de la terre (1964)
André Miquel, La géographie humaine du monde musulman jusqu'au milieu du 11e siècle, vol. 1 (1967)

journey.event: Born in Nisibis in upper Mesopotamia (modern Nusaybin) into a merchant family.
Set out from Baghdad on 15 May 943 (7 Ramadan 331 AH), beginning thirty years of travels.
Crossed northern Mesopotamia and recorded Mosul in his section on al-Jazira.
Visited Egypt during the late Ikhshidid period, several decades before the Fatimid conquest.
Reached the Aghlabid-Fatimid capital of Ifriqiya, beginning his documented North African circuit.
Visited the great Saharan caravan terminus that linked the Maghrib to the gold trade of West Africa.
Recorded Fez under the late Idrisids before it passed to the Umayyads of Cordoba.
Reached the Strait of Gibraltar before crossing to al-Andalus.
Visited the Umayyad capital of al-Andalus, leaving an admiring account of its size and prosperity, while noting the Umayyads' military vulnerability.
Visited Kalbid Sicily, leaving the most detailed surviving Arabic description of Muslim Palermo.
Reached Armenia, recording the local languages and Christian populations.
Travelled through the Caucasus, where he claimed to have heard 360 languages spoken.
Returned south through Hamdanid Syria, recording Aleppo and Antioch.
Travelled across Azerbaijan during a period of Buyid-Hamdanid border conflict.
Recorded Buyid Isfahan in his section on Fars and the Jibal.
Visited the Buyid capital of Fars under Adud al-Dawla.
Stopped at the great Persian Gulf port of Siraf, hub of the Indian Ocean trade.
Reached the Indus valley, recording Multan and giving one of the earliest Muslim geographical descriptions of Sind.
Visited the Samanid capital of Transoxiana.
Recorded Samarkand and its famous gardens trimmed in the shape of animals.
Visited the Khwarazmian capital before turning toward the Caspian.
Recorded the Khazar capital on the Volga shortly before its destruction by Sviatoslav of Kiev.
Returned to Baghdad after his eastern circuit.
Returned to Sicily for a second visit, where he wrote his famous (and polemical) account of Palermo.
Completed Kitab Surat al-Ard, drawing on Istakhri's earlier work and his own thirty years of travel.
Last attested writing dates from after 978; place and exact year of death unknown, conventionally placed around 988 in or near Baghdad.
